one_line_takeaway: A broad single-author taxonomy/survey of LLM hallucinations that usefully consolidates types, causes, benchmarks, and mitigations under a 'hallucination is inevitable' thesis borrowed from cited theory, but presents no original experiments and is a trustworthiness/reliability review rather than a security contribution.
key_finding: The report's organizing thesis (adopted from cited theoretical work) is that hallucination is an innate, inevitable property of any computable LLM regardless of architecture, training data, or prompting, so the practical objective must shift from elimination to robust detection, mitigation, and human-in-the-loop oversight. It argues no single technique suffices and advocates hybrid, context-aware systems combining tool use, retrieval grounding (RAG), fine-tuning, and symbolic guardrails, plus interface-level mitigations (calibrated uncertainty displays, source-grounding indicators) because cognitive biases (automation bias, confirmation bias, illusion of explanatory depth) cause users to overtrust fluent-but-false outputs even when warned.
core_contribution: A single-author survey/report (Manuel Cossio, Universitat de Barcelona; arXiv:2508.01781, cs.CL) that synthesizes the existing literature into a comprehensive, multi-layer taxonomy of hallucinations in large language models. Its own contribution is organizational rather than empirical: it consolidates (1) definitions and the theoretical 'inevitability' framing, (2) two core dichotomies (intrinsic vs. extrinsic; factuality vs. faithfulness), (3) ~13 specific manifestation types (factual/fabrication, contextual, instruction, logical, temporal, ethical, amalgamated, nonsensical, and task-specific: dialogue, summarization, QA, code, multimodal), (4) a three-bucket cause model (data-, model-, prompt-related, ~20 factors), (5) cognitive/human-factors influences on hallucination perception, (6) a survey of detection benchmarks and metrics, (7) architectural + systemic mitigation strategies, and (8) web-based dashboards for monitoring model reliability. The synthesis is crystallized in three summary tables (Table 1 theorems, Table 2 taxonomy of types, Table 3 root causes). It presents no new model, dataset, or experiment of its own.
threat_model: Not a security paper and it offers no formal threat model of its own. The dominant frame is reliability/trustworthiness of NLP output, not adversarial security. Security-adjacent material appears only as minor subsections that the author reports from cited work: (a) 'adversarial attacks' where deliberately or inadvertently fabricated details embedded in a user prompt induce the model to elaborate false information ('garbage in, garbage out'), plus a noted 'threat of malicious misuse' where bad actors exploit LLMs to spread falsehoods (secs. 4.1.3, 5.3.1); and (b) a passing observation that code-generation hallucinations can introduce security vulnerabilities (sec. 7.1.5, CodeHaluEval). No attacker capabilities, trust boundaries, or defenses-vs-adversary are formalized. Included in the AI-security knowledge graph as background on LLM trustworthiness/hallucination, not as a security contribution; no threat model was invented for this review.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no original evidence, model, or evaluation, so its taxonomy is a synthesis whose validity rests entirely on the cited literature. It repeatedly notes the field lacks a standardized definition of 'hallucination,' making the taxonomy's own categories overlapping (e.g., intrinsic vs. faithfulness are acknowledged to overlap) and cross-study numbers non-comparable. The 'inevitability' theorems are asymptotic/worst-case results borrowed from one cited paper and do not quantify real-world rates. Section 9 (Artificial Analysis, Vectara leaderboard, Epoch AI, LM Arena) is largely descriptive of web dashboards with dated screenshots (June-July 2025) rather than a systematic comparison, and several of these tools admittedly do not measure hallucination directly (using reasoning/accuracy as proxies). Single-author, no stated systematic-review protocol (no search strategy, inclusion criteria, or PRISMA-style methodology).

datasets_used: No datasets used by the author (literature survey; no experiments). Benchmarks it SURVEYS/describes (all cited, not run): TruthfulQA (adversarial general-knowledge QA); HalluLens (taxonomy-aware, multi-context); FActScore (entailment-based summarization factuality); Q2 and QuestEval (QA-based consistency); MedHallu (10,000 QA pairs derived from PubMedQA); MedHallBench; Med-HALT (Reasoning + Memory hallucination tests); CodeHaluEval (input-/context-/fact-conflicting code hallucinations); HALLUCINOGEN (VQA object-hallucination benchmark for LVLMs); KILT (knowledge-grounded factuality).
quantitative_results: REPORTED FROM CITED SOURCES, not the author's own measurements: logical inconsistencies account for ~19% of identified hallucination cases; temporal disorientation ~12%; ethical violations ~6% (sec. 4.4/4.5/4.6).
MedHallu comprises 10,000 high-quality QA pairs derived from PubMedQA (cited, sec. 7.1.5).

Epoch AI dashboard trends (cited, sec. 9.3): ~+12 percentage points on GPQA Diamond and ~+17 pp on MATH Level 5 for every 10x increase in training compute; models below ~10^24 FLOPs often near/below random on GPQA Diamond.

Open-vs-proprietary gap (Epoch AI, cited): in Jan 2025 OpenAI o1 beat best open model Phi-4 by ~20 pp on GPQA Diamond and ~29 pp on MATH Level 5; best open LLMs lagged best closed by ~6 months (GPQA) to ~20 months (MMLU); DeepSeek-R1 narrowed the MATH L5 gap to ~2 pp behind o3-mini and ~4 pp behind o1 on GPQA Diamond.
LM Arena descriptive stats (cited, sec. 9.4): ~70% of monthly prompts are fresh; >40% of battles involve an open model.
baselines_compared: None. No experimental comparison is performed by the author. The paper contrasts categories of DETECTION METRICS descriptively (surface/embedding similarity: ROUGE, BLEU, BERTScore vs. classifier/NLI-based: FactCC, SummaC vs. QA-based: Q2, QuestEval vs. knowledge-grounded: KILT, RAE) and categories of MITIGATION (Toolformer-style tool augmentation, RAG retrieval grounding, synthetic/adversarial fine-tuning, symbolic guardrails/logic validators/factual filters/rule-based fallbacks), all attributed to cited work.
